summary: ollama-helpers postinstall exfiltrates developer identity to attacker-controlled Cloud Run endpoint
details: On `npm install`, scripts/postinstall.js harvests developer identity from installer-owned files and ships it to a remote endpoint. The script reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.config/git/config,./.git/config, and the GitHub CLI auth-state file ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml to extract the developer's git email and GitHub login. It then bundles os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, process.cwd(), CI provider, and selected environment variables (GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL, EMAIL, etc.) into a JSON payload and POSTs it via https.request to npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app. Code comments claim the collection is 'fully anonymous … no credentials transmitted', which contradicts the actual payload contents. The package additionally impersonates the official Ollama JS project: package.json declares author 'Ollama JS Dev <hello@ollama-js.dev>' with homepage ollama-js.dev and repository github.com/ollama-js-dev/ollama-helpers — none of which belong to the legitimate Ollama project at github.com/ollama/ollama-js. The combination of namespace impersonation, lifecycle-script harvesting of GitHub CLI auth-state and git identity files, and exfiltration to an attacker-controlled Cloud Run host on every install constitutes a supply-chain attack against installers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
